claims_verified.claim: A write-time OSError no longer leaves a derelict empty file at the intended slot
claims_verified.evidence: note_server/writer.py:79-89 now wraps the fdopen/write OSError handler with a best-effort os.unlink(path) before re-raising WriteError. Live repro: monkeypatched os.fdopen to raise OSError after os.open succeeded — write_note raised WriteError and os.listdir(d) was empty afterward (previously showed a lingering 'notes.md').

claims_verified.claim: A retry after a write-time failure reuses the original name instead of silently drifting to the next suffix
claims_verified.evidence: Live repro continuation: after the cleaned-up failure, a second write_note(d, 'notes', 'second attempt') call returned 'notes.md' (not 'notes-2.md'), confirming the slot was freed.

claims_verified.claim: New tests cover both the cleanup and the retry-reuse behavior
claims_verified.evidence: tests/note_server/test_writer.py:78-116 — test_write_note_wraps_write_time_os_error now asserts list(tmp_path.iterdir()) == [] after the failure; new test_write_note_failed_write_does_not_shift_retry_to_suffix asserts a subsequent write_note call returns 'notes.md' and that 'notes-2.md' does not exist

test_assessment: My prior blocker (orphaned empty file + silent name drift after a write-time OS failure) is fully resolved and regression-tested at both the cleanup and retry-reuse levels.
residual_risks: The os.unlink cleanup is itself best-effort (wrapped in its own try/except OSError: pass) — if the unlink also fails (e.g. permissions changed mid-request), the derelict file could still linger in a rare double-failure scenario, but this is an acceptable residual edge case given the primary failure is already reported to the caller.
